title: SC decisions on the economy
sub_topics: Article XII Section 10 — Filipino preference clause
40% foreign ownership computation on voting shares
Ateneo Law School lecture by Azcuna

stances.claim: In Manila Prince Hotel v. GSIS, the majority subverted the essence of public bidding — the constitutional Filipino-preference clause applies only when 'the scores are tied,' not when 'the ballgame is over and the foreigners clearly posted the highest score.'
stances.rhetorical_move: self-citation of his dissent + sports metaphor

stances.claim: His December 1, 2004 La Bugal-B'laan resolution correctly upheld the Mining Law, regulations, and FTAA because the Charter expressly authorizes the President to enter into FTAAs with foreign corporations and impliedly allows foreign partners management covenants, provided ultimate full control remains with the President.
stances.rhetorical_move: self-citation of his majority resolution authorship + textual reading of Article XII

stances.claim: Festering policy volatility has stunted Philippine mining growth — mineral exploration requires long gestation and huge venture capital, so unless government assures credible and stable long-term policies that are not changed midstream, investors will remain lukewarm.

The originalist vs liberal interpretive debate is not confined to local jurists — US Justice Scalia's frank refusal to speak at the Global Forum because he interpreted only the US Constitution (without international law) illustrates the originalist commitment, while Justice Kennedy welcomed the international engagement.
stances.rhetorical_move: personal anecdote with named Justices used as illustrative contrast

institutions: Global Forum on Liberty and Prosperity
cases: La Bugal-B'laan v. Ramos (January 27 and December 1, 2004)
events: Global Forum on Liberty and Prosperity (October 18-20, 2006)

signature_phrases: when the scores are tied. But not when the ballgame is over and the foreigners clearly posted the highest score
unignorable realities of globalization and deregulation
Mr. Chief Justice, you do not really want me there
register_markers: It reminded me of my column last April 14
decision_framework_signals: applies originalist vs liberal-interpretation framework as the meta-question for constitutional economics cases
frames investor confidence as dependent on policy stability — translates legal volatility into economic outcomes
uses bidding/sports-metaphor framing to argue Filipino preference is a tiebreaker, not a trump card
uses comparative-justice anecdotes (Scalia originalism vs Kennedy international engagement) to illustrate interpretive philosophies
cross_references: my column last April 14 which discussed the two main ways of constitutional interpretation
notable_anecdotes: Inviting Scalia to speak at the 2006 Global Forum on Liberty and Prosperity — Scalia's frank refusal because his oath was to the US Constitution alone, with verbatim quote
Kennedy agreeing to speak via video teleconferencing despite committing to another country on those dates
